items.correct: The small projecting line at the end of letter strokes.
items.misconception: A whole typeface that looks formal.
items.why: Students conflate the FEATURE (the small foot/stroke) with the CLASSIFICATION (serif typefaces, which have them). Sans serif means 'without serifs' - those little projections at letter endings.

items.q: What is the difference between relief and intaglio printing?
items.correct: Relief prints from raised surfaces; intaglio prints from incised lines below the surface.
items.misconception: They are different names for the same process.
items.random_wrong: Relief is for paper, intaglio is for fabric.
items.why: Students don't grasp the inverse logic. Lino cuts AWAY non-printing areas (relief - raised inked); etching incises lines that HOLD ink below the surface (intaglio - ink pressed up into paper).

items.q: What were Impressionist painters primarily trying to capture?
items.correct: The fleeting effects of light and atmosphere at a particular moment.
items.misconception: Their feelings or first impressions of a scene.
items.why: Students take 'impression' as personal feeling. Impressionism was about visual perception - how light actually appears on surfaces, painted quickly outdoors before light changed.

items.q: Which designer is famed for using negative space in mid-twentieth-century film posters?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Saul Bass. “Paul Rand” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which colour model is standard for full-colour print production?
items.why: It's CMYK. “RGB” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many ink colours does CMYK printing use?
items.why: The real figure is 4 colours. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
